To the clients of Mittleman Value Partners

Peter Lynch on investing: “The person that turns over the most rocks wins the game.” Recent market action compelled me to turn over more rocks than usual, which delayed my year-end 2025 letter into the Q1 2026 combo I present today. I hope the extra transparency provided makes it worth the wait.

2025: The rep account gained 19% net of fees in 2025, vs. 17.9% for S&P 500, 12.8% for Russell 2000, and 22.9% for MSCI ACWI; all total returns. Our low in 2025 was -15% on the 4/8/2025 market low of the tariff tantrum and our high for 2025 was over +25% in early September, with a weak USD assisting.

2026 Q1: The rep account dropped 4.59% net of fees in Q1 2026, vs. a loss of -4.35% for the S&P 500, a gain of 0.92% for the Russell 2000, and a decline of -3.10% for MSCI ACWI; all total returns. Simply put, the place to be in Q1 2026 was natural resources, with the Bloomberg Commodity Index +23.3%. Not having exposure to that segment of the market, along with a stronger USD, hindered our results in Q1.

Still, if the value-based investment discipline to which I adhere is valid and my ability as a practitioner sufficient, the idiosyncratic aspects of our portfolio should far outweigh the various macro headwinds or tailwinds over time. So, while this highly global (82% of portfolio is non-U.S.) approach lately seems dominated by the ups and downs of the USD currency, with the strong USD weakening our unhedged results in Q1 2026, I do not expect our returns to be perpetually pressured in the absence of USD weakness. Yet, I remain a bit wary of most USD assets, mainly on valuation, as the positioning implies.

Artificial Intelligence (A.I.) is top of mind in markets around the world in 2026, and it may well live up to the expectations supporting the unprecedented amounts of capital being invested in developing it and the high valuations awarded to the perceived leaders, companies both public and private.

Then again, I recall after the COVID crash of March 2020, the video-conferencing software company Zoom (ZM) (NASDAQ: ZM, $79) soared from $67 on 12/31/19 to $589 on 10/23/20 on rampant expectations that we'd all be using Zoom (and seemingly nothing else) instead of business travel going forward. And while Zoom did grow tremendously, at its $589 peak the valuation was $173B, more than triple the combined market caps of the big four major U.S. airlines (DAL, UAL, AAL, LUV) at about $50B around that time. Today the market cap. of Zoom is $25B while those four airlines combined are over $100B. People continue to fly for many business meetings, despite the ease of jumping on a Zoom call.

I think a similarly wrongful extrapolation is creating opportunities today in companies misperceived as being doomed by the competition that A.I. may enable. In most cases, I think these companies will be beneficiaries, not victims of the great things that A.I. can do for us all. A.I. should more likely enhance H.I. (Human Intelligence) rather than replace it. The overdone selling for fear of disruption from A.I. was exacerbated by another source of concern which is the war with Iran which began on 2/28/26. The mild market sell-off that ensued made some of the names on my watch list cheap enough to buy.

As highlighted in the table above, six new names entered the portfolio during Q1 2026 and early Q2, totaling nearly 19% of the portfolio value at cost. That unusually concentrated cluster of new entrants is oddly very similar to what occurred in early 2025, when it was mainly the tariff-tantrum that triggered the bargains. On the next page a snapshot of the portfolio from a valuation perspective shows just how jaw-droppingly cheap the shares of those highlighted new holdings appear.

As shown below, the portfolio overall is extraordinarily inexpensive at a weighted average 6.0x EBITDA. It's not just our small cap holdings that are super cheap, and maybe as such more understandably so; even our large caps like Bayer AG (at $46B it's the largest market cap. in the portfolio after Alibaba (BABA) was sold in 2025 at $154 on average, a $368B mkt. cap.) is still only 7.7x EBITDA for 2026 (estimated USD 10.5B excluding $500M SBC), when peers trade at 10x. Getting to 10x EBITDA would increase Bayer's ADR (BAYRY) from $11.75 currently to $17.90, another 52% upside. We sold some BAYRY at $13.17 and $14.17 in the rep acct. in Q1 2026 to make room for some of the new holdings.

I plan to describe the investment thesis for each of the new holdings in my next letter, but feel free to inquire directly via phone or email if you would like to know more sooner. I expect they will become larger weightings in the portfolio as this year progresses.

The biggest contributor to our 19% gain in 2025, AMA Group Ltd., with nearly 4 percentage points of contribution from a 54.1% gain, was also the biggest detractor from performance YTD as of 4/10/26, with its 34.4% YTD loss costing 3.24 percentage points of performance, without which our -2.25% YTD loss through 4/10/2026 would have been a 1% gain.

At roughly AUD 0.50 (USD 0.35) now, AMA's stock price is valuing Australia and New Zealand's largest collision repair firm by far, with over AUD 1B in sales, at a measly enterprise value of AUD $235M (excluding leases), which is only 3.1x the EBITDA (after lease expenses) of AUD $75M expected in calendar 2026. I think fair value is triple the current price, which would be an enterprise value of 9.5x EBITDA for c2026 (AUD $75M), and 8.4 EBITDA for c2027 (AUD $85M).

AMA's share price was declining well before the Iran conflict began, from an interim peak of AUD 1.10 (USD 0.72) in October 2025, despite producing steadily improving results in-line with their forecasts and analysts' expectations. The pre-war weakening of the share price might have been due to sellers taking profits from the huge issuance of new shares back in July 2024 at a split-adjusted AUD 0.42, which while dilutive did at least put the company in a nearly debt-free (excluding leases) position.

AMA dropped nearly 30% in AUD terms, -32% in USD, during the month of March as the war in Iran caused energy prices in Australia and New Zealand to surge, which the market correctly fears might lead to reduced miles driven (and thus fewer collisions to repair) if it continues for much longer. Australia is unusually vulnerable in this regard, given almost all of its fuel is imported, and they have very little in reserves. But even if gas lines form and rationing ensues, it should be months not years.

There were long gas lines near where I lived in Old Brookville, New York, in 1979 due to the Iranian revolution shutting down Iran's oil production back then, and the Iran vs. Iraq war that followed. I was 11 years old and waiting for an hour, sometimes more, in the back of my Mom's car for our turn to get gas. Cars with even-numbered license plates could get gas on even-numbered calendar days, and odd numbered only on odd dates. But like the initial gas crisis in 1973-1974, it only lasted a few months.

Insider buying at AMA Group, as with a few of our other holdings like Mattel, has been encouraging. AMA director Brian Austin most recently bought another 1.5M shares at AUD 0.614, more than 22% above the current price, on March 10 th & 12 th . He now owns just over 10M shares.

Mattel's Chairman and CEO, Ynon Kreiz, bought another 65,000 shares at $15.53 ($1.79M) on 2/12/26, given him 1,794,217 shares ($26M) in total now. Mattel Films' " Masters of the Universe " movie will be released on June 5, 2026, and hopefully He-man and Skeletor can create a merchandising boom like Mattel's " Barbie " movie did in 2023.

And shortly after we bought our new position in Italy's dominant payments firm Nexi Spa (NEXPF) (NEXI: IM) at €2.85 (USD 3.27) on 3/18 and 3/20, Nexi promoted from within a new CEO, Bernardo Mingrone, who bought 327,868 shares at €3.02 (just under €1M) on 3/26, the day he was appointed and spoke of a focus on capital returns, which is encouraging. At our cost Nexi's stock has a 10% dividend yield with a 20%+ free cash flow yield.

After briefly being surpassed by Canadian bus maker, NFI Group (NFYEF) (NFI:CA), as our largest holding, Aimia Inc. (AIM:CA) is back to our largest weighting as I sold some NFI Group in Q1 into its recent rally to fund new holdings. Aimia's stock price was flat (0% return) YTD through 4/10/26, and almost flat in 2025 save for a weakening USD which gave the CAD denominated shares a boost in USD terms.

Aimia had a bit of a setback in Q1 when it announced the sale of its largest asset, the Italian specialty chemical company Bozzetto, which Aimia agreed to sell for much less than the analysts who cover Aimia and I thought it was worth. I had estimated that Bozzetto was worth a minimum of 8x EBITDA but it was sold for what appears to be about 6.5x EBITDA, an enterprise value of C$411M with net proceeds to Aimia of C$265M. That reduces my NAV per share estimate for Aimia from C$5.00 to C$4.00, which while painful is still 47% higher than the C$2.73 closing price on this Friday, Apr. 10 th .

On the bright side, the sale of Bozzetto might not have happened at all had Aimia held out for a better price, as the war with Iran which broke out less than three weeks after the Bozzetto sale was announced has caused input costs to soar (crude oil, naptha, etc.) so the timing was fortuitous. Getting Aimia some cash to invest, potentially during a bear market cycle (the sale should close in May), might yield much more than what seems to have been left on the table in the Bozzetto sale. Sometimes it is necessary to take a step back before attempting a bigger leap forward.

After a rough start, 2025 turned out well for our investment performance. I think by again having been prepared to take advantage of opportunities as they present, usually quite inconveniently and sometimes all at once, we have set the stage for 2026 to be even better.

I look forward to sharing more details about the new holdings soon and again I welcome any inquiries in the interim.

Sincerely,

Christopher P. Mittleman, Chief Investment Officer

Mittleman Value Partners LLC

data.title: CGO: Positioned For Continued Outperformance Of Foreign Markets
data.description:
The Calamos Global Total Return Fund (CGO) is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a high level of both capital appreciation and current income. The fund does reasonably well in terms of current income, as its 7.64% current yield is higher than most alternatives that investors might consider for income along with global equity exposure. Unfortunately, though, this fund’s performance against global equity indices has been rather disappointing, as it has been underperforming in recent years. With that said, though, this fund’s performance has been good enough that investors who care more about income than total return might still be willing to consider it. Its performance has also been improving in recent months, which might be an encouraging sign. The global exposure here is also nice, as foreign markets have been outperforming American ones over the past year or two, and as such, investors with foreign exposure have enjoyed greater wealth accumulation. Let us look more closely at this fund in order to determine whether or not it is worth considering for new money today.

About The Calamos Global Total Return Fund

The website for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund states that it has the primary objective of providing its investors with a high level of capital appreciation and current income. The fund seeks to achieve this objective by investing in what its manager describes as a “broad range of security types:”

This is not really surprising for a closed-end fund managed by Calamos Investments. As investors who are familiar with this fund house are likely aware, Calamos funds frequently include a combination of common equities, convertible bonds, and high-yield bonds, among other financial instruments (such as bank loans, preferred stock, and occasionally structured products). The Calamos Global Total Return Fund is no exception to this, as its website provides the following asset allocation for the fund as of March 31, 2026:

As this chart may be difficult to read, here is the same information in table format:

Asset Type

% of Total Assets

Common Stock

61.28%

Convertibles

19.64%

Corporate Bonds

7.18%

Cash and Receivables/Payables

4.17%

Sovereign Bonds

2.64%

Other

2.51%

Options

1.57%

Bank Loans

0.98%

We can see that there is certainly a fairly wide variety of asset types here, and this is certainly not unusual for this fund. A core aspect of Calamos Investment’s strategy is to use whatever assets the management believes will allow the fund to deliver the highest possible total return, regardless of what types of securities they are. With that in mind, we might wonder why the fund does not just invest entirely in common stocks. After all, most financial websites and financial advisors consistently state that the stock market outperforms all other financial assets.

In fact, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has outperformed common stocks since the start of the 21st century. On December 29, 2000, which was the last trading day for gold in the twentieth century, the yellow metal closed at $272.65 per ounce. The closing price on April 22, 2026, was $4,729.44 per ounce. That is a 1,634.62% return since the start of the century. On December 29, 2000, the S&P 500 Total Return Index (SP500TR) was at $1,807.13. The index closed at roughly $15,930 on April 22, 2026. That is a 781.51% total return for common stocks since the start of the 21st century, assuming that all dividends paid out by all of the constituents of the S&P 500 Index were reinvested over the period. Clearly, common stocks have not been the best-performing financial assets over the past 25 years. However, as is the case with most closed-end funds, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund does not invest in physical gold. It only invests in securities such as common stocks and bonds. It can, however, include shares of gold mining companies. The fund had 0.99% of its portfolio invested in Alamos Gold (AGI) as of February 28, 2026, for example. However, unlike a fund such as ASA Gold & Precious Metals Limited (ASA), investing in gold miners is not the primary focus of this fund.

At this point, one might once again suggest that a portfolio consisting entirely of common stocks should deliver the highest returns out of all types of securities. Historically, that has been true over extended periods of time, but there have been periods in which other types of securities have outperformed common stocks. We can actually see this simply by looking at the performance of various types of securities over the past decade. This chart shows the performance of the S&P 500 Total Return Index, the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT), and the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK) over that period:

Over the ten-year period, common stocks did deliver the highest total returns. However, prior to the COVID-19 pandemic era, we can see that the total return performance of common stocks and convertible bonds was almost identical. In 2020 and 2021, convertible bonds were actually outperforming common stocks by a fairly significant margin. In addition, historically, certain types of bonds have actually outperformed common stocks during recessions and panics. As such, while it is true that common stocks tend to be the highest-performing financial securities over the long-term, there are other assets that can do better during certain economic or market environments. This is one reason why financial planners typically recommend that individuals do not simply invest entirely in common stocks as a way of saving money for retirement. A portfolio consisting of various types of financial securities can actually be advantageous to own over the long term because of the fact that one type of security can do well when another is performing poorly. In addition, we cannot always predict in advance which asset will do the best. For example, there were not very many people back in April 2023 who predicted that gold would outperform both the S&P 500 and large-capitalization technology stocks (XLK) over the next three years.

As such, it can be a good idea to run a diversified portfolio with exposure to several different asset types. This approach appears to be consistent with the strategy under which the Calamos Global Total Return Fund operates.

The largest positions in the fund’s portfolio also show the fund’s general diversification across asset types. The website provides the following list of the fund’s top ten holdings as of March 31, 2026:

We see a combination of common stocks, convertible bonds, and even a convertible preferred security issued by Boeing (BA.PR.A). This reinforces the fact that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is not an equity fund that holds an allocation to fixed-income securities like some other closed-end funds that we have discussed in this column. Investing in convertible securities is a core component of this fund’s strategy. In fact, Calamos Investments is one of the largest convertibles management companies in the United States, and most of its funds include a hefty allocation to convertible securities. The best-performing funds from this manager, however, have a portfolio that has more than half of its assets invested in common stocks alongside a sizable convertible sleeve. This combination still provides the fund with most of the upside potential of common stocks, along with a higher income than it would earn from a portfolio that consists entirely of common equities.

There are some changes in the fund’s portfolio since we last looked at it a year ago. Several of these changes are for the better. For example, the fund’s largest positions list no longer includes the SPDR Blackstone Senior Loan ETF (SRLN) or the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). The SPDR Blackstone Senior Loan ETF was not even in the fund’s portfolio at all on February 28, 2026, but BKLN still accounted for 0.49% of its assets at that time. These securities were both 2.3% of the fund’s net assets on March 31, 2025, so it appears that the fund has been divesting itself of these positions. I will admit that I like this move, which is not likely to be surprising to regular readers. As I have pointed out in past articles (such as this one), senior loans tend to be a very poor asset to hold during monetary easing periods. The latest guidance from the Federal Reserve predicts that the effective federal funds rate will be at 3.4% by the end of 2026 and 3.1% by the end of 2027, which would require a single 25-basis-point interest rate cut in both years:

The central bank does not predict any increases in the federal funds rate in the near future, although the market is actually priced for a very small chance of a 25-basis-point increase this year:

As of right now, the market thinks that there is a 0.4% chance that the Federal Reserve will increase interest rates this year, but the majority opinion still very much favors either no change or a cut by the end of the year. The market and the Federal Open Market Committee both strongly believe that there will be one or two 25-basis-point cuts by the end of next year. Thus, despite some concerns that sprang up last month that the rising oil prices will force the Federal Reserve to switch back to a monetary tightening regime, neither the market nor monetary policymakers believe that. The overwhelming consensus points to continued monetary easing.

Bank loans are not a good asset class to hold whenever a nation’s central bank is easing monetary policy. This is because these securities usually have a variable-rate coupon that pays out an amount of money that is determined by short-term interest rates. In the United States, the yield of these securities is generally equal to the average Secured Overnight Financing Rate plus a specific percentage. The Secured Overnight Financing Rate itself tends to very closely correlate with the federal funds rate. Thus, whenever the Federal Open Market Committee cuts interest rates, the Secured Overnight Financing Rate declines, and the coupon payments paid out by bank loans decrease. Unlike bonds, bank loans do not rise in price whenever interest rates decline. As such, the attractiveness of bank loans as holdings decreases in response to monetary easing. As the Federal Reserve is widely expected to continue to ease going forward, CGO almost certainly has better options than holding ETFs comprised of bank loans. The fund’s management made a good decision divesting these securities.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund also reduced its exposure to Southern Company’s (SO) and Spotify USA’s (SPOT) convertible bonds, as well as Amazon’s (AMZN) and JPMorgan’s (JPM) common stock since the last time that we looked at it. In their place, we have Samsung (SSNLF), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Hyundai Motor (HYMLF), Iberdrola Finanzas (IBDSF), ASML Holding (ASML), and UniCredit S.p.A. (UNCFF). The addition of Samsung Electronics is not particularly surprising, as Samsung has been one of the best-performing common stocks in the market over the past year or so. Samsung Electronics is a major producer of computer memory, which has benefited from a surge in demand as various technology companies construct data centers and computer hardware to support their efforts in developing artificial intelligence. This surge in demand has actually caused a shortage of certain types of memory and driven up the prices of this vital computing component. Samsung Electronics’ common stock is up 290.48% on the South Korean KRX exchange over the past year.

This has greatly outpaced most other stocks in either American or global markets over the same period. As a result, any fund that owned it prior to the start of this year (it is up 81.40% year-to-date) has seen its representation as a proportion of assets increase. As such, many global closed-end funds have Samsung among their largest positions in the portfolio right now.

The incredibly strong performance of Samsung over the past several months may understandably raise questions about how sustainable the current price is, and how well the stock will be able to hold onto its gains. After all, the rapid appreciation was caused by a shortage of electronic components, and shortages rarely last forever. Back in January, the Consumer Electronics Show was held in Las Vegas, Nevada, and this event gave memory purchasers the opportunity to discuss the shortage with suppliers (PC World published an article mid-January discussing this event). Based on these discussions, it is very possible that the current memory shortage will continue through at least the end of 2027. The markets are forward-looking, so it is quite possible that Samsung’s stock will begin to decline once investors have confidence that the current tightness of memory supply will begin abating. However, that still gives the Calamos Global Total Return Fund plenty of time to realize the gains that it has earned in this stock. There is no guarantee that it will be successful in timing its exit, but it still might start taking some gains off of the table before the memory shortage is resolved.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund Versus Index ETFs and Peers

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund has a 7.64% yield at the current share price. This yield compares favorably to the yields of any of the major broad-market American or global equity indices. We can see that in this chart:

These two indices are, of course, the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. As we can see in the chart above, both of these indices have far higher yields than the ones that include American stocks. This strongly suggests that foreign stocks have, on average, higher yields than American ones. Further evidence of this can be found by looking at the yields of the country-specific indices of those countries that are included in the portfolio of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund. The fund’s website provides the following country allocation as of March 31, 2026:

Here are the yields of the country-specific indices and exchange-traded index funds tracking each country’s common equity market:

As clearly shown, all of the non-American country-specific index ETFs have higher yields than the one tracking American equities, except for the one tracking Japanese stocks. This supports our previous assertion that non-American stocks have higher yields than those issued by companies based in the United States. This suggests that income-seeking investors may wish to have a higher level of exposure to non-American stocks than most of the global indices do, as the higher yields that are available outside of the United States will allow an investor to earn more current income given a specific amount of principal.

The Calamos Global Total Return Fund could be following this suggestion. As the chart above shows, the fund has 47.7% of its assets invested in the United States. This would, naturally, give it a 52.3% allocation to foreign assets. This is higher than either the MSCI World Index or the MSCI All-Countries World Index possesses:

As shown, both of these indices, which are supposedly global, have less than 40% of their value in non-American stocks. That is far out of proportion to the representation of the United States in the global economy (the United States only accounts for about 27% of global gross domestic product), which could suggest that the American equities in general are overpriced relative to the rest of the world. The Calamos Global Total Return Fund is a bit more balanced in this respect, although it also has an outsized exposure to the United States relative to its actual representation in the global economy. The more important thing here, though, is the higher yields that non-American stocks have relative to American ones. As this fund has a higher level of exposure to non-American assets than the indices, the effective yield that it earns from its equity portfolio should be higher than the indices earn. The fact that this fund also holds convertible bonds and junk bonds, which also both have higher yields than common stocks, should result in its portfolio having a higher effective yield than any of the global equity indices. This should prove to be appealing to just about any income seeker.

The fund’s American exposure has come down a lot since the last time that we looked at it. At the time that my previous article was published, this fund had 57.30% of its assets invested in the United States. This suggests that this fund has been benefiting from the outperformance that foreign markets have been delivering over the past year or so. This chart shows the performance of American equities (VTI) against the ACWX Index since the start of 2025:

As we can see, the international index fund is up about 40.64% since the start of 2025. This was a far better performance than the 21.19% gain that American equity indices delivered over the same period. The fact that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has a higher level of exposure to foreign markets than the indices do should have been a benefit for it in this situation because it was better positioned to capture foreign market gains. While this fund did have greater American exposure the last time that we discussed it, the 57.30% weighting towards the United States that CGO had in April 2025 was still lower than the indices.

The fund’s higher level of international exposure relative to the global indices also should have benefited the fund so far this year. This chart shows the price performance of the Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF against the ACWX index year-to-date:

As we can see, the outperformance of foreign markets relative to American ones is still ongoing. It was not a one-time event. As CGO is more heavily weighted to foreign stocks than domestic ones, this should position it well against the indices.

The fund’s positioning has indeed allowed it to deliver superior performance. Since the start of 2026, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has delivered an 11.41% total return, beating both VTI and ACWX:

However, this fund did not do quite as well in 2025. This fund has only managed to deliver a 22.46% total return since the start of 2025. That was pretty similar to the performance of VTI over the same period, but the fund greatly underperformed the MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index:

It is possible that the junk bonds in the portfolio of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund held it back last year. The fact that the fund had greater exposure to American equities in the early stages of 2025 might have also been a drag on its performance. There are some other funds on the market that have outsized exposure to non-American common stocks and similar bond exposure that substantially outperformed VTI since the start of 2025. For example, the Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust (TBLD) has delivered a 49.03% total return since the start of 2025:

Regardless of the cause of the fund’s underperformance versus ACWX, though, it does appear that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has altered its positioning to take advantage of continued outperformance of foreign stocks. The fact that the fund has beaten both indices year-to-date stands as evidence of this.

Of course, we should note that this same change could hurt the fund should American stocks once again start to outperform those issued by entities located outside of the United States. I will admit that I do not see a catalyst for such a change to the current trend, but most estimates of gross domestic product growth do have the United States delivering greater growth than the majority of developed market nations over the next year.

While the 7.64% yield of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is quite attractive compared to most index funds, it is not nearly as attractive when compared to closed-end funds that typically have much higher yields. We can see that in this peer comparison chart:

The median yield out of this group of ten hybrid closed-end funds is 9.10%, which is quite a bit higher than the current yield of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund. This may be disappointing for investors whose primary goal is to generate a high level of current income. After all, the higher the yield of a given fund, the higher the amount of income that it will provide. There are several peer funds here that provide their investors with higher amounts of income than CGO.

With that said, Calamos closed-end funds tend to be lower-yielding than other closed-end funds on the market, but they deliver stronger capital appreciation in exchange. It is not unusual to see a fund from this fund house have a low yield compared to similar funds but deliver higher total returns. The Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ) is a good example of such a fund, as it only has a 7.60% yield, but it has actually beaten the S&P 500 Total Return Index over the past decade. As such, we should compare the trailing ten-year total return performance to that of its peers. This chart provides the numbers:

While not the best performer, we can see that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund did manage to deliver a pretty good showing over the trailing ten-year period. The above numbers assume that an investor in each of the funds used the distributions to purchase more shares on the distribution payment date, so we can clearly see that CGO’s lower yield relative to its peers did not really handicap the fund’s ability to provide its investors with a respectable return.

Recent Performance

The last time that we discussed the Calamos Global Total Return Fund was on April 30, 2025. As such, almost a year has passed since the last time that we looked at this fund. We have already discussed some of the important developments that have occurred since that time that actually affect this fund, so let us just have a look at the fund’s performance since that time.

Shares of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund are up 20.31% since our discussion, underperforming the MSCI All-Countries World Index:

However, if we take the distributions paid out by this fund over the period, we see that it very slightly outperformed the index:

The fund’s outperformance was not consistent, though. As shown, there were several time periods during which this fund was underperforming the index. That may not be surprising given its different composition. As already mentioned, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has exposure to both convertible bonds and junk bonds that have different return profiles than global common stocks. The fund has sufficient exposure to these securities to alter its portfolio’s performance. In addition to this, CGO uses leverage, which can potentially enhance returns, but it also increases the fund’s volatility. We can see that in the chart above, as CGO tends to experience larger declines than does the index whenever common stock prices decline.

Distribution Analysis

The website for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund states that the fund has the primary objectives of providing its investors with both capital appreciation and current income. In accordance with the current income objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.08 per share ($0.96 per share annually). This gives the fund a 7.64% yield at the current share price. This fund has historically had a reasonably stable distribution, as the last time that it changed was back in October 2022, when it cut the payout by $0.02 per share monthly. It has kept it stable at $0.08 per share per month since that time. This may be nice for those investors who are looking for a stable source of income, but we should still have a look at the fund’s finances to see just how sustainable it truly is.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report for the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is the annual report for the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025. This is a newer report than the one that was available to us the last time that we looked at this fund, so let us review this document and see how well CGO did at covering the payout.

For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund reported a net investment loss of $1,270,969. This was obviously not enough to cover any distribution, but the fund still paid out $9,434,202 to its investors over the period.

Fortunately, this fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on October 31, 2025, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund reported net realized gains of $10,766,298 along with net unrealized gains of $12,862,989. The fund’s investment portfolio produced investment profits of $22,358,318 over the period:

This was more than the fund paid out in distributions over the period. In addition, this fund managed to cover its distributions solely through net realized gains despite the net investment loss. For the twelve-month period, the total of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund’s net investment loss and net realized gains was $9,495,329. That was more than the $9,434,202 that the fund paid out. Thus, it managed to cover its distributions just with its realized profits, albeit barely. Still, though, this is the ideal situation, and as such, it does not appear that this fund is distributing more than it can actually sustainably afford.

Valuation

Shares of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund are currently trading at a 0.96% discount on net asset value. This is a substantially less attractive price than the 10.30% discount on net asset value that this fund’s shares have had on average over the past year.

The current price of this fund is also somewhat less attractive than its long-term average. Over the past five years, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund has traded at prices ranging from a 12.00% discount to a 23.81% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period has been a 2.97% discount on net asset value. As the five-year average is a larger discount than this fund has today, we can assume that it might be possible to wait a little bit for a more attractive price to emerge.

Here is how the current price of the Calamos Global Total Return Fund compares with its peers:

One thing that we immediately notice is that most of these funds are trading at prices that are actually higher than the intrinsic value of their assets. The Calamos Global Total Return Fund is one of the few exceptions to this, as it is at a slight discount. There is only one fund out of the peer group that is currently trading at a cheaper price (the Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust). While this does point to the fund being reasonably priced relative to its peers, that does not necessarily mean that it is a good buy right now. As we have already seen, CGO has historically traded at larger discounts than the fund has today. In this light, it might be best to wait until the discount widens before purchasing shares.

Expenses

The fund’s most recent annual report states that the Calamos Global Total Return Fund had an expense ratio of 4.02% in the most recent period. However, as is the case with most closed-end funds, the bulk of this fund’s expenses comes from the fact that it uses leverage and has to pay interest on the money that it has borrowed. If we exclude these expenses, the fund’s expense ratio was 1.78% for the most recent period.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Calamos Global Total Return Fund is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a way to get exposure to non-American common stocks along with a higher level of income than most index funds can provide. The fund is not the highest-yielding one around, though, as there are other global hybrid funds with higher yields. This fund appears to be reasonably well-positioned to profit should foreign assets continue to outperform American ones, as the fund is underweight to the United States relative to the indices. This has likely been a contributing factor in this fund’s exceptionally strong performance year-to-date. However, the current price is less attractive than this fund’s typical price, and as such it might make sense to wait for a better entry point if you want to purchase the fund.

data.title: Grey Owl Capital Management Q1 2026 Client Letter
data.description:
“The reason gas prices are so high is because the oil is in Texas and Oklahoma and all the dipsticks are in Washington.”

- Unknown

Dear Client,

The Grey Owl All-Season Strategy’s objectives are to minimize drawdowns, outperform short-term bonds by several hundred basis points each year (i.e., beat “cash”), and participate meaningfully in risk-on rallies.

In the first quarter of 2026, we, again, demonstrated an ability to minimize drawdowns during a meaningful risk-off period. The Nasdaq-100 Index1 was hit the hardest of the major indices. It dropped -13% from January 28 through March 30, 2026. The first month of the drop, from late January to late February, appeared catalyzed by decelerating earnings for artificial intelligence-linked firms. Equity ownership has crowded into these names, so when they turned, the selling fed on itself. Through the end of February, global equities2 continued to make new highs. Then the United States and Israel launched a war against Iran and equities broadly declined for the next month. The Grey Owl All-Season Strategy remained positive on a year-to-date basis during this entire period. Subsequently, the strategy joined (though, as expected, less aggressively) the April equity rally.

Despite different catalysts and narratives, this pattern looks like the beginning of 2025. Recall that the popular “Magnificent 7” group of stocks declined roughly -30% from its December 17, 2024 peak to the April 8, 2025 low3 . During that period, the Grey Owl All-Season (GOAS) portfolio was down less than -3% at its worst point in early April of 2025. The rebound that followed was rapid, and the year ultimately proved strong for most risk assets. GOAS managed risk during the drawdown and then repositioned to participate as conditions turned risk-on.

We don't know if the remainder of 2026 will play out similarly to 2025. Our objective remains to construct an all-season portfolio that can make steady progress in all (or most) environments and to tilt it toward the prevailing conditions. As we write, a fragile ceasefire with Iran remains in place, but unresolved — the Strait of Hormuz blockade continues, and oil is still well above pre-war levels. Yet, economic and corporate earnings growth remains strong, and inflation is once again accelerating. From our perspective, this calls for a balanced portfolio with a cautious bent.

A few specifics below on the present environment and our current positioning, but first a more detailed review of the performance of the “primary” asset classes.4

For the first quarter of 2026, commodities were the big gainers. War in the Middle East drove oil from under $60 to a peak above $115 before settling near $90. This sharp move rippled through all global assets. Commodities shot up +39.9% and gold gained +8.6%. Bonds were flat — seemingly torn between their “risk-off” nature (a “flight-to-safety”) and the accelerating Consumer Price Index (CPI)5 presaged by the rapidly accelerating price of oil: US Treasury Bonds were +0.2%. Global equities finished down -2.2% and U.S. equities were hit harder, down -4.4%. Over this period, GOAS delivered a +1.7% return.

Economic Growth

Hedgeye's real GDP projection model continues to show a reacceleration in growth through the first half of 2026. Growth is then set to moderate as the third quarter looks to be slightly slower than the previous year. The fourth quarter's pace is forecasted to just modestly beat 2025. Absent a re-escalation of war in the Middle East and/or a prolonged stalemate that traps Middle Eastern oil, we expect risk assets to continue performing well while this acceleration holds.

Economic growth is accelerating, which historically supports risk assets—particularly cyclical equities and commodities.

Inflation

On the other hand, Hedgeye’s CPI model forecasts significant and consistent CPI acceleration.

Inflation pressures are increasing, creating a backdrop that favors some risk-assets over others.

Market Signals

Lowry’s analysis showed an intense and rapid pickup in selling pressure during the first quarter of 2026. That pickup has abated since early April, which is encouraging, but a few weeks is not enough to confirm the all-clear, and Lowry's own longer-term indicators still signal caution.

While the current trend of increasing Buying Power and decreasing Selling Pressure is positive, there are more subtle indicators that have yet to flash “risk-on.” Lowry’s writes, “News aside, the weight of evidence is starting to land for the bulls, and fresh all-time highs in several important price indexes certainly can brighten the mood. However, the Lowry Analysis needs just a bit more confirmation from our longer-term indicators to reflect sustainable intermediate-term strength.”

Market internals support the case for a continued advance, but longer-term indicators have yet to confirm sustainable strength.

Current Positioning

Our current portfolio remains balanced within an all-season framework but is less aggressive than in January. Since last quarter, we have increased fixed income and cash allocations. Gold and commodity allocations decreased (though very recently after the big ramp in oil and the multi-year ramp in gold). Our US equity exposure is more heavily weighted to energy today, but was even larger a month ago – we locked in some gains. Global equity exposure is more concentrated in Latin America and Asia than in Europe. At the highest level, the specific holdings are unconventional, but the aggregate risk exposure lines up almost perfectly with a 60% equity / 40% bond benchmark.

This positioning maintains meaningful protection against inflation or market stress while attempting to capture growth where it is most likely to be rewarded by the predominant environmental conditions. The larger fixed income and cash position is optionality, not a view — it lets us deploy into weakness if the ceasefire breaks, even as we accept the drag while we wait. This balance—rooted in our all-season philosophy and adjusted for present conditions—reflects our core belief: don’t try to predict the future; position with prevailing conditions while diversifying to enable success across many possible futures.

The portfolio remains balanced but is intentionally tilted toward capital preservation.

*****

As always, if you have any thoughts regarding the above ideas or your specific portfolio that you would like to discuss, please feel free to call us at 1-888-GREY-OWL.

Sincerely,

Grey Owl Capital Management, LLC

1 We use the ETF QQQ to represent the Nasdaq-100 Index.

2 We use the ETF ACWI (the MSCI ACWI - All Country World Index) to represent global equities.

3 We use the ETF MAGS to represent the “Magnificent 7” stocks.

4 We refer to US equities, long-dated US Treasury bonds, gold, and commodities as “primary” asset classes borrowing the language of HCWE & Company. These four assets best capture two variables that explain a significant amount of asset price movement: global growth and inflation. This framework is the basis for a permanent portfolio, an “all-season” portfolio, risk-parity, etc. US equities and commodities are “risk” assets, while US Treasury bonds and gold are “haven” assets. Returns are measured on a total return basis using index exchange traded funds (ETFs): SPY for the S&P 500, ACWI for the MSCI All-Country World Index, GSG for the S&P GSCI Commodity Index, TLT for 20+ Year Treasury Bond index (i.e. “long-dated” US Treasury bonds), and GLD for gold.

5 We specifically use the government statistic CPI to try to avoid a theoretical discussion around inflation. Regardless of whether there is true inflation from monetary debasement, when the oil price increases, it flows through to CPI, and bond yields adjust.

data.title: Planet Labs Brings Edge AI To Orbit Using Nvidia: More Upside Ahead
data.description:
Thesis

Again, my bull case for Planet Labs (PL) really hinges on them successfully making the jump from a data provider to a real-time geospatial intelligence platform. The progress we’re seeing is huge and is also starting to convert into some very strong stock momentum, with shares now trading near the $40 mark. I think if the Pelican constellation, especially assets like the Pelican-4, can scale AI-powered, in-orbit analytics, then Planet is in a very good position to materially increase the value of each image sold. What they want to deliver is not just raw data but rather actionable insights as fast as possible. That shift should open the door to higher-margin, recurring revenue streams, particularly from government/defence clients, which is where we are seeing a lot of the recent tailwinds.

On top of that, we have an excellent combination of high revisit rates, improved resolution, and now onboard AI, via hardware like Nvidia Jetson Orin. This sets up Planet to build something closer to a global monitoring network than a traditional satellite business. I think if execution holds, this could drive a lot of strong operating leverage over time, with software-like economics layered on top of their already fixed satellite infrastructure.

In my previous coverage, I went over Planet's last earnings and its path to profitability. Back then, shares were trading in the $33 range, and the valuation profile was appearing very stretched. However, shares have run up another 17% since then, and the space intelligence industry is seeing some very favourable tailwinds with geopolitical tensions rising.

Redemption of public warrants

We recently saw Planet announce the redemption of some public warrants. Now, what Planet is essentially doing here is forcing the conversion or expiration of its remaining public warrants. They gave holders the right to buy shares at a fixed price of about $11.50. And since the stock has been trading well above the $18 threshold required to trigger redemption, the company can now call these warrants in and redeem them for a nominal $0.01 if holders don’t act. In practice, this should push warrant holders to either exercise at $11.50. I see this as a bit of a balance sheet and capital structure clean-up. They’re set to remove overhang from potential future dilution, and it also simplifies the equity structure somewhat. And at the same time, if holders do choose to exercise before the deadline, Planet simply receives additional cash from those conversions, which will help out the liquidity position.

AI detection in space

Now, you may have seen that Planet has successfully run an AI object detection model on its Pelican-4 satellite. I see it as a very impressive result, and what Planet has actually demonstrated here is a shift from satellites being simply passive cameras in space to becoming active, intelligent systems. The goal here is for their satellite fleet to be able to interpret what they see in real time. Specifically, it's their Pelican-4 line-up, and it didn’t just capture an image over Alice Springs; what it did was process that image onboard using an AI model to detect aeroplanes at an airport within moments of capture. You see, traditionally, satellites collect raw imagery and transmit it back to Earth, where ground-based servers run analysis, which can take hours or longer depending on bandwidth and processing queues.

That ground-based analytical chain will also often use AI models, but in this case, the AI inference/object detection happened directly in orbit. They used an embedded computing module, the NVIDIA Jetson Orin. As for the results, there was about 80% detection accuracy on raw imagery, which is actually pretty notable given the constraints of space hardware. Keep in mind that this is all happening on the back of a satellite in space, so there’s very limited power, radiation exposure, and also thermal challenges from the satellite working too hard. Basically, this means the satellite could, for example, immediately flag the presence of things like aircraft, ships, or vehicles without needing to send the full image first. If we think about this, the implications or uses in certain sectors in a few years time are huge.

As for the role of Nvidia, it’s pretty central here since the Jetson platform gives them compact, high-performance AI computing, specifically designed for edge environments. So that would be places where data is generated and must be processed locally. Things like drones, robots, and now satellites. And by integrating this kind of hardware, Planet here is effectively giving its Pelican-4 a brain to complement its visual edge. Keep in mind that, in terms of imaging performance, Pelican-4 is in its high-resolution class. It’s tipped to provide imagery at about 40 cm resolution, so that means each pixel represents about 40 cm on the ground, with orthorectified products typically delivered at around 50 cm per pixel. That would make it right up there in terms of the best in class, and is also detailed enough to clearly distinguish objects like vehicles, aircraft, and infrastructure layouts. Now, because of this kind of resolution, they’re able to identify things like planes at an airport via AI. You may also be aware that the Pelican-4 boasts a very impressive revisit rate. A single Pelican satellite can actually image the same location every 4 to 5 days or so on its own, but if we place this inside the wider Pelican constellation, coverage improves dramatically here. It’s designed for multiple revisits per day globally, up to about 10x, and even higher at mid-latitudes. So in practice, that means users can monitor some very fast-changing situations with near-continuous updates.

So overall, this should reduce the need to downlink massive datasets. Rather, the aim would then be for the satellite to send much smaller, more information-rich outputs. Possibly, just as coordinates, object counts, or alerts. For example, 5 aircraft were detected at this specific location. Now, these implications are significant, especially for things like disaster response. Satellites could essentially identify flooded areas or damaged infrastructure within minutes, and for the defence side, the possibilities are quite endless. I think the Pelican line, along with Planet’s upcoming Owl constellation, is now more so aimed at clients who need rapid, actionable intelligence. So governments and defence agencies, as I’ll explain.

Pelican-4 positioned for defence

The way I see it, the Pelican-4 and the wider Pelican constellation here are clearly being positioned with defence and intelligence customers in mind, not just commercial imagery buyers. What I see as specifically different is that Planet Labs is no longer selling pictures of Earth, but rather real-time intelligence products. These kinds of offerings are far more aligned with military needs. They now have onboard AI demonstrated on Pelican-4, which allows the satellite to detect objects like aircraft in orbit and then simply send alerts within minutes rather than hours. For defence users, time compression is a major part of paying up for insights. An alert about aircraft appearing on a runway or vehicles moving near a border is operationally extremely useful, whereas a delayed raw image is not. And this is why recent contract growth is being driven very heavily by government/defence customers. Their backlog has hit around $900 million and is growing rapidly.

Now, if we compare the Pelican to some of Planet’s earlier satellites like Dove or even the SkySat, the Pelican here is much more suited to defence, in my opinion, for three key reasons. First, it has that much higher resolution and much higher revisit rate I mentioned. So they can offer persistent monitoring rather than just occasional snapshots. Second, has to be the AI-narrative. Going forward, we can expect it to be able to process data onboard using systems like Nvidia Jetson instead of relying on ground analysis. This is what allows for a near-real-time surveillance asset. Third, I see the overall design more as part of a networked constellation, with frequent revisits, up to multiple times per day globally, which is essential for tracking any kind of dynamic situations. A lot of earlier satellites were optimised for broad, daily imaging, which was useful for agriculture or mapping, but now tailwinds have clearly shifted. This is why we’re seeing Planet put so much time and effort into expanding its satellite portfolio. The Pelican is optimised more so for speed, responsiveness, and actionable insight.

Valuation

The company holds roughly a $13 billion market cap, and it’s no secret that Planet is being valued much more like a high-growth software/AI company than a traditional aerospace or imaging business. Some of their multiples are screening as extremely overvalued with the recent run-up to the $40 share price, and because the company is not yet profitable, the most relevant metrics here are revenue-based multiples. That being said, the stock is trading at around 38x trailing price-to-sales and about 42× EV to sales, compared to a sector median closer to just 2x. Even forward multiples are pretty high at around 30x, which would suggest investors are willing to pay a hefty premium here for anticipated future growth rather than the current earnings or cash flow. Cash flow and book value multiples are also pretty stretched, so clearly this is a long-duration growth story rather than a fundamentals-driven valuation.

I think what the market is essentially pricing in is a big transition from satellite imagery provider to real-time geospatial intelligence platform. Now, this can come in a number of ways, and one big feature will be their AI-driven capabilities, like those demonstrated on Pelican-4, seen as a catalyst. And since the involvement of AI in satellite imagery data is still a new and emerging field, it can be hard to price in just how much value they can offer going forward. So this kind of valuation now would imply expectations of rapid revenue scaling, strong operating leverage, and eventual high-margin, recurring contracts. The latter here is mainly tipped to come from defence/government customers. In other words, here, investors are betting that Planet can evolve into a critical infrastructure layer for global monitoring and decision-making, but data, analytics, and speed are going to command far higher margins than competitors only offering raw imagery.

There's also a lot of high short interest in the stock we have to address. As of late March, this year, about 41 million shares were sold short, which makes up between 14% to 14.4% of the public float. Now, I think at this kind of level it's generally considered elevated and would tell me that a meaningful portion of the market is betting against the stock. I also think this is likely due to its stretched valuation and execution risk.

Valuation risk in a competition context

I think it’s worth quickly lining up Planet against some peers like BlackSky (BKSY) and Spire Global (SPIR). The valuation gap here becomes pretty hard to ignore. Planet is trading at about 38x price-to-sales and 42x EV to sales, whilst BlackSky sits closer to 12x sales and 14x EV sales. Spire, although they’re quite a bit behind in terms of constellation build out, is even lower at around 8x sales and 9x EV to sales. So even within their specific sector, which already trades at a premium, Planet is looking valued at a multiple several times higher than its peers.

What I’ve found interesting is that BlackSky’s stock price is actually very similar in absolute terms to Planet’s at that $40 range, and their run-ups often mirror each other. I think a lot of institutional players looking for space intelligence exposure are taking positions in both, and that's even with BlackSky having a much smaller constellation and overall scale. It goes to show how little the share price itself tells you. Planet’s valuation here is far richer once you factor in the larger market cap at $13 billion vs. $1.4 billion for BlackSky. Spire, meanwhile, is even smaller and trades at a significant discount, which would reflect a more diversified but less defence-focused data model.

I think what makes Planet look unfavourable on valuation is that a lot of the future upside already appears priced in to a lot of investors. The market is clearly baking in the successful execution of their AI-driven, real-time intelligence strategy. They’re also putting a lot of strong growth into high-margin government and defence contracts. By comparison, BlackSky and Spire offer a similar thematic exposure but at much lower multiples, which tells me that they require less perfection in execution to justify their own valuations. So, in short, yes, Planet may have the most advanced vision, but it also has the least room for error at current pricing.

Competition and potential impact of SpaceX IPO

I also think it's worth taking a look at what a potential IPO from SpaceX would likely do for Planet. I see it as somewhat of a double-edged catalyst for Planet here. On one hand, I see it bringing some significant investor attention and capital into the space sector. In that sense, it would help validate the broader space/data/AI, which comes with a lot of operators in the segment. So that increased institutional interest could benefit Planet indirectly here, especially if we consider that a lot of investors look for pure-play public comps in geospatial intelligence.

On the other hand, there could be some downside in that it would also raise the competitive bar significantly. As you know, SpaceX, via its Starlink infrastructure and launch dominance, certainly has the ability to vertically integrate a few capabilities. Things like data collection, transmission, and potentially some kind of analytics aspect could be something they pursue post IPO. And if this kind of expansion gets further into Earth observation or real-time intelligence, that could compress margins and increase pricing pressure across the industry.

Looking ahead

Again, my buy thesis here is that Planet becomes a mission-critical intelligence layer for Earth. I feel demand is structural, especially in defence and climate supply chains, and contracts should be sticky and long-term. The advancements in AI should also meaningfully expand both use cases and pricing power. And at the current valuation, which already reflects a lot of this, if we see them deliver, I think more upside comes from proving they can scale it.

data.title: Recycled Yield: DeFi's Circularity Problem
data.description:
The current problem with DeFi is that the organic demand for on-chain credit, borrowing to fund productive economic activity, trading, or real liquidity needs, is far smaller than the supply of capital seeking yield. That imbalance is what the wrapper-and-loop machinery exists to fill.

On April 18, 2026, an attacker forged a cross-chain message and extracted 116,500 rsETH, roughly $292 million, from Kelp DAO's bridge. The tokens were not sold. They were posted as collateral on Aave, where the attacker used them to borrow real ETH (ETH-USD), extracting genuine assets against worthless collateral, leaving the lending protocol facing $124–230M in potential bad debt and $6–8B in withdrawals over 48 hours.

The event will be catalogued as a bridge failure and a collateral-design failure, both correct. The structural question is why a single asset breaking on a single L2 route was enough to put a multi-billion-dollar lending market into a liquidity crisis. The answer sits upstream of the exploit, in a structural problem the industry has been working around for years: the genuine yield that Ethereum and DeFi produce is thin, and the organic demand for on-chain credit is shallow.

Every layer of the stack - issuers, lenders and depositors - has an incentive to manufacture additional yield where the underlying activity doesn't generate it. The arithmetic of how that yield is actually constructed explains both the boom and the transmission path.

The Base: Real Sources of DeFi Yield

DeFi does have genuine yield sources. Three mechanisms produce real cash flow: staking rewards paid by proof-of-stake networks, interest paid by borrowers on lending protocols, and trading fees paid to liquidity providers.

For ETH-denominated strategies, staking is the dominant source. Native ETH staking produces identifiable cash flow from three sources: newly issued ETH (protocol issuance, loosely comparable to seigniorage), priority fees from users transacting on the chain, and MEV, value captured from ordering transactions. With roughly 39M ETH staked across 1M validators as of early 2026, the reference rate sits near 3% APR.

Ethereum Staking Reward Rate

This is genuine cash flow paid in the network's native asset. It is the closest thing DeFi has to a risk-free rate, a sovereign-like yield denominated in ETH.

Lending interest is the second major source and the one most relevant in this article. When a borrower draws ETH from a lending protocol like Aave, they pay interest to the supplier. Supply APYs on ETH lending pools typically run 1–4%, depending on utilization. The important subtlety — explored below — is that most of the borrow demand on these markets comes from loopers recycling the same staking base, which makes the "organic" yield on ETH lending partly self-referential.

Ethereum staking yield can be viewed as the risk-free rate in ETH-denominated DeFi strategies. In traditional credit analysis, a spread above the risk-free rate is attributable to credit risk, duration, liquidity, or leverage. The same discipline applies here, but DeFi spreads are rarely labelled honestly.

The Wrapper Stack

Understanding the leverage requires understanding the wrapper ecosystem that makes it possible. DeFi's yield stack is literally a sequence of tokens, each of which is a claim on the one below it, each tradable and re-pledgeable independently.

  • Layer 0 — Staked ETH. A validator locks ETH into Ethereum's staking contract and earns the 3% base rate. Capital is committed directly to the protocol; there is no receipt token at this layer, and the ETH is illiquid until withdrawn.

  • Layer 1 — Liquid Staking Tokens (LSTs). Protocols like Lido and Rocket Pool user ETH, run the validators on their behalf, and issue a tradable receipt token — stETH, rETH — that accrues the staking yield. The LST is the breakthrough that made staking composable. A holder has both staked yield exposure and a liquid asset that can be sold, traded, or pledged. stETH alone backs roughly $7B of collateral across DeFi.

  • Layer 2 — Liquid Restaking Tokens (LRTs). EigenLayer allows ETH (or LSTs) to be "restaked" — pledged simultaneously as security for other protocols in exchange for additional fees. LRTs like Kelp's rsETH, EtherFi's weETH, and Renzo's ezETH are receipts for LST deposits that have been deposited into EigenLayer. They inherit the LST's staking yield, add a modest restaking premium, and remain tradable and pledgeable themselves. Each LRT is a wrapper around a wrapper: a receipt for a restaked position on a receipt for a staked position on underlying ETH.

  • Layer 3 — Collateral on a lending market. The LRT is deposited on DeFi lending protocols like Aave as collateral. The lending market assigns it a loan-to-value ratio and allows the depositor to borrow other assets against it, most commonly ETH itself.

Each layer by itself is a piece of financial engineering: a liquid receipt for an illiquid position, a way to earn more on the same capital.

Below flowchart shows the complete stack:

  • User deposits ETH → gets an LST (via Liquid Staking Protocol like Lido or Rocket Pool).

  • Then LST Restaking or Native Restaking into EigenLayer.

  • EigenLayer delegates to AVSs (Actively Validated Services) for extra yield.

  • You receive an LRT (liquid restaking token like rsETH) that stays tradable while earning both base staking + restaking rewards.

The Loop

A user holding rsETH posts it on Aave (AAVE-USD) as collateral. Because the borrow rate on ETH is below the effective yield of rsETH, the user borrows ETH, stakes it back into rsETH, and redeposits. The new collateral allows another borrow, which funds another stake, which becomes more collateral. The position can be geared three or four times before the health factor on Aave becomes too tight to continue.

The arithmetic is straightforward. If the base is 3% and the loop is geared four times, the gross yield on the original capital is roughly 12%. The spread over the borrow cost, say 8%, is the quoted "APY" of the strategy.

But no new cash flow is being generated in this process. The 3% is counted once as validator rewards, again as the stETH holder's yield, again as the rsETH holder's yield, and again as the looper's yield (with leverage). The same underlying ETH cash flow is being claimed by multiple wrappers, and the looper is claiming a multiplied version of it.

Why this layer is the fragile one. Three properties of the loop make it the transmission mechanism for any shock upstream.

First, the loop is actually the marginal buyer of LRT supply. Organic demand for rsETH, for holders who want yield without looping, is a fraction of total LRT supply. Most LRT issuance is absorbed by looping positions on lending markets. When looping demand retreats, LRT supply has no natural bid.

Second, the loop is one of the largest sources of borrow demand on ETH lending markets — and this is where the circularity becomes important. Some research finds that recursive leverage accounts for roughly 20% of total borrow volume on Aave V3, with concentrations running materially higher in LST and LRT pools. Protocol data from Morpho and Spark puts looping at 30–64% of positions in key correlated-asset markets. In other words, lending interest, genuinely paid by real borrowers, is a legitimate DeFi yield source, but a substantial share of the borrowers paying that interest are loopers recycling the same staking base.

Bottom Line

DeFi is not a Ponzi — there is real underlying value being leveraged, and both staking rewards and lending interest are genuine cash flows. But the system is self-referential in a specific sense: the borrow demand that produces the "organic" supply APY on ETH lending markets is itself largely driven by loopers farming the spread against the staking base. The yield looks like it comes from two independent sources (staking rewards plus lending interest), but a meaningful share of the lending interest is paid by participants whose only economic purpose is to recycle the staking yield at higher gearing.

The problem with DeFi is that the organic demand for on-chain credit, borrowing to fund productive economic activity, trading, or real liquidity needs, is far smaller than the supply of capital seeking yield. That imbalance is what the wrapper-and-loop machinery exists to fill.

data.title: U.S. Transaction Volumes Rise +27% YoY In Q1 2026
data.description:
Rich Hill, Senior Managing Director - Global Head of Real Estate Research and Strategy

Real Capital Analytics data released on Thursday showed U.S. commercial real estate transaction volumes accelerating meaningfully in early 2026. Most notably, 1Q26 transactions totaled $135.8bn, up 27% YoY, despite a volatile macro backdrop.

March volumes rose 40% YoY, while January and February were revised sharply higher to +24% and +18%, respectively, evidence that activity was materially stronger than initially reported.

Breadth across property types reinforces our view that private real estate remains fully in recovery. Six of seven sectors posted higher YoY volumes in 1Q26, led by Senior Housing (+194%), Hotels (+64%), Office (+39%), and Industrial (+27%).

Retail (+3%) and Apartments (+1%) also remained modestly positive. Data centers (-31%) were the lone decliner, reflecting the sector’s inherently lumpy, large-ticket transaction profile rather than a deterioration in fundamentals.

While some deals were likely agreed upon prior to March’s geopolitical volatility, their completion is a clear positive and underscores improving market confidence.

Transaction volumes tend to act as a barometer for sentiment: accelerating volumes signal growing demand and confidence, whereas slowing volumes would suggest increasing caution amongst investors.

Importantly, this strength aligns with both the preliminary 1Q26 NCREIF ODCE returns of +1.24%, marking the seventh consecutive quarter of positive performance, and with the U.S.-listed REIT market returning to prior cycle highs, a classic leading indicator of market expansion.

Bottom line

Taken together, Thursday’s transaction volume data reinforce our view that the U.S. private real estate cycle remains fully in recovery, supported by open and liquid debt markets.

Historical cycle analysis suggests recoveries average roughly 2 years, followed by long, durable expansion phases lasting 12–13 years, driven not only by price appreciation but also by underappreciated income growth1. The current cycle continues to unfold in largely textbook fashion, suggesting it remains early by historical standards.

1. For further analysis, read The (CRE)covery

data.title: U.S. Flash PMI Signals Inflation Spike As Supply Concerns Fuel Stockpiling, But Help Boost Growth
data.description:
The flash US PMI survey data from S&P Global indicated a muted rebound in business activity in April, after growth had almost stalled in March on the outbreak of war in the Middle East. However, part of the rebound reflected a short-term boost from stockpiling as companies feared supply shortages emanating from the conflict.

Not surprisingly, prices are already spiking higher in this environment, and not just for energy but for a wide variety of goods and services. The overall inflation picture is now the most worrying for almost four years.

Output rebounds after stalling in March

The headline flash S&P Global US PMI Composite Output Index rose from a two-and-a-half-year low of 50.3 in March to a three-month high of 52.0 in April. The improved reading signals faster economic growth at the start of the second quarter, albeit running well below the highs seen last year.

Moreover, we have seen the weakest expansion of output recorded since the start of 2024 over the past three months, with the war in the Middle East squarely to blame, according to survey respondents.

The April PMI reading is broadly consistent with the economy struggling to manage annualized growth in excess of 1%.

Service sector malaise

Service sector activity remained especially subdued. Although services output recovered slightly from a dip in March, the rate of expansion remained the second-weakest recorded over the past 14 months due to a further cooling of demand growth. New business placed with service providers, ranging from travel and tourism to financial products, rose only marginally and at the slowest rate seen over the past two years, led by an ongoing decline in exports of services.

Lost sales were commonly linked by survey contributors to the uncertainty and disruption caused by the war in the Middle East alongside other government policies and affordability issues, with the prospect of higher borrowing costs acting as a further deterrent to spending.

Manufacturing stockpiling

In contrast, the manufacturing sector saw output rise at the sharpest rate in four years, fueled by the largest influx of new orders since May 2022. However, both output and new orders growth were boosted by stock building amid concerns over supply availability and price hikes due to the ongoing war.

Some survey respondents reported “panic” and “emergency” buying in echoes of the problems seen during the pandemic. The rise in orders was driven by domestic demand, as export sales of goods fell at an increased rate.

Supply chain delays

War-related issues led to increasingly widespread supply problems, adding to existing supply delays, which have been largely blamed on tariffs. Factories reported the greatest lengthening of supplier delivery times since August 2022, with lead times having now lengthened continually over the past eight months.

In addition to shipping-related disruptions due to the war, shortages were also linked to the additional purchasing of safety stocks. Purchasing activity rose at the second-fastest rate seen for nearly four years, beaten only by the jump in buying activity seen shortly after last spring’s tariff announcements.

Prices spike

Average prices charged for goods and services rose in April at the fastest pace since July 2022 amid increases in input prices and supply scarcities. The rise in prices is consistent with a marked upturn in the rate of consumer price inflation in the US.

While manufacturers reported an especially steep jump in goods prices, with the rate of inflation at a ten-month high, service sector selling price inflation also accelerated to reach a 45-month high.

Manufacturing input costs, meanwhile, surged especially steeply, rising at the fastest rate for ten months and posting the second-steepest increase since July 2022, while the rise in services costs was the largest since December and among the sharpest seen over the past three years. Alongside higher energy prices, companies reported increased charges for a broader swathe of commodities and inputs, as well as citing increased staffing costs.
