data.title: Trend Following's Bond Problem
data.description:
Our own Jeff Malec went solo on the Derivative last week, dishing on all sorts of topics from ski races to conferences. But one topic caught our eye in particular: talking bonds.

And particularly bonds in times of war. Last month, when the U.S. began military strikes on Iran, markets delivered a reminder that the old playbooks do not always apply.

Equities sold off. Oil surged higher. And U.S. Treasuries, historically one of the market’s go-to safe havens during geopolitical stress, declined as yields moved sharply upward.

In a moment where many expected a classic “risk-off” response, bonds instead participated in the volatility. The 10-year Treasury yield moved from roughly 4.0% toward 4.3%, after already pressing near 4.5% earlier in the year before retracing. The prevailing interpretation was straightforward: higher oil prices could revive inflation pressures, prompting markets to quickly reprice future interest rates.

That may be logical on paper. In real time, however, watching bonds sell off while missiles were in the air for an Iran war many had thought would be a prelude to World War III was a striking departure from conventional expectations.

A Historically Difficult Period for Bonds

But this episode was not an outlier. It was simply the latest chapter in what has become one of the most challenging stretches for fixed-income investors in decades.

Across a few bond ETF proxies, the damage is easy to spot:

For investors accustomed to bonds serving as ballast, this has been a painful adjustment.

Why This Matters for Managed Futures

But this isn’t just a story of bonds not going up (rates down). Here in our alternative investment world, heavy in trend following, global macro, and managed futures, it’s more that they haven’t been going anywhere.

Bonds have historically been one of the most important return drivers for managed futures and trend-following strategies. Review many of the strongest CTA periods, from the late 1980s through the 2000s and into the Global Financial Crisis, and the trading of fixed income futures (in rates and bonds) frequently played a leading role.

Here are the best 5 years in the SocGen Trend Index over the last 24 years, and you can see that bonds played a big part, contributing between 25% and 96% of the total performance. (Note: This is using the Trend Indicator’s bond performance vs. the Trend Index performance, so not exactly correct, but close enough for these purposes).

Importantly, this is not limited to U.S. Treasuries. Managed futures programs typically trade a broad universe of fixed income markets across the globe, from Australia to Canada, and Germany to Japan.

These markets are deep, liquid, and often trend together during major macro shifts. When bonds trend cleanly, they can provide meaningful opportunities across multiple regions simultaneously.

The issue recently has not been a lack of movement. It has been the lack of sustained movement. Instead of durable trends, bond markets have been characterized by reversals, false starts, and range-bound trading for much of the last five years.

The Numbers Behind the Frustration

2022 actually demonstrated how powerful bond trends can still be. As inflation surged and yields rose rapidly, trend-following managers were able to position short bonds and capture one of the more profitable CTA environments in recent memory. As seen above, the SocGen CTA Index posted one of its strongest annual returns during that period.

Since then, however, the environment has changed dramatically. Markets have struggled to determine whether rates should continue materially higher or gradually drift lower. That indecision has created repeated whipsaws for systematic trend strategies.

Using SocGen trend indicator data, again, the results are striking:

That is not simply below average. It is historically extreme. For investors frustrated with recent managed futures performance, bond market behavior is likely the primary explanation.

Gains from trends in commodities (oil, anyone?), currencies, and select other sectors have often been offset, or fully erased, by losses within fixed income positioning.

Roy Niederhoffer’s Warning Looks Prescient

A few years ago on The Derivative, Roy Niederhoffer discussed an important structural issue that now feels highly relevant. His argument was that the 30-year bull market in bonds created a powerful tailwind for many systematic strategies. Falling yields and rising bond prices offered persistent, clean trends.

But simply reversing that chart, assuming rising rates and falling bond prices would be equally profitable, was never guaranteed. Why?

Because shorting bonds introduces different economics:

  • Roll costs can become a headwind
  • Carry dynamics change
  • Downtrends may be less persistent
  • Choppier price action can disrupt systematic signals

In other words, correctly forecasting higher rates does not automatically translate into profitable trend-following returns. That distinction matters. A manager can be directionally right and still struggle if the path is noisy enough. That has largely defined the post-2022 bond market.

So Where Does That Leave Investors?

We do not believe this environment lasts forever. Bond markets will eventually establish a new equilibrium. And when they do, trend-following strategies should be well positioned to respond, whether the next durable move is higher yields, lower yields, or a globally divergent rate regime.

Fixed income remains a core sector in managed futures portfolios for a reason: these markets historically do trend, and when they do, the opportunity set can be substantial.

But the current drought is real. It has been historically severe, unusually persistent, and likely the single largest drag on CTA returns in recent years.

Final Thought

No strategy works in a straight line, and managed futures is no exception. Understanding why recent performance has been muted is often the first step toward maintaining conviction through difficult periods. In this case, the explanation may be simpler than many realize: It has largely been the bond market.

data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of April 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the April 2026 edition of the lithium miner news.

The past month saw lithium prices significantly higher.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported an Australian spodumene concentrate 6% Li₂O CIF China spot price of USD 2,485 as of April 23, 2026, significantly higher than the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was significantly higher last month, now at CNY 173,000.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5-year chart - CNY 173,000 (~USD 25,342) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

Katusa Research is forecasting a small lithium deficit to begin in 2026 (as of January 2026) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, and may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid-2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025)

Trend Investing vs. IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

Lithium market and battery news

On March 23, Mining Visuals reported:

Lithium market balance: Visualizing the shift from surplus to deficit by 2026...Prepare for the 2026 shortfall: Acknowledge the consensus among major financial institutions that lithium will enter a structural deficit by 2026, with estimates projecting a shortage of up to 80,000 tonnes.

Several analysts are forecasting a lithium deficit in 2026 (source)

On March 25, Car News China reported:

CATL’s domestic EV battery share reaches 50.1% in Q1 2026...China’s total EV battery manufacturing volume reached 310 GWh in the first two months of 2026, up 22% year-over-year...BYD reached a share of 17.5% in the first quarter of 2026, down 4.3% year-over-year.

On April 9, Caspar Rawles (Benchmark Mineral Intelligence) posted on LinkedIn:

The lithium "buffer" is officially gone. While the market expected a structural build-up in 2026, we are seeing the exact opposite. Chinese lithium inventories have plummeted to their lowest levels since May 2024.

On April 13, CNEVPOST reported:

CATL weighs $5 billion Hong Kong share sale, report says...CATL has also been considering selling convertible bonds to meet some of its funding needs.

On April 14, Africa Business Insider reported:

Africa’s top lithium producer eases export restrictions, introduces quotas for Chinese firms...Chinese firms have quickly secured special export quotas, allowing them to resume lithium shipments from Zimbabwe. China’s Sichuan Yahua Industrial Group said it has obtained a six-month export quota for lithium concentrates from Zimbabwe, just two months after Harare halted exports of the key battery material...Yahua, while speaking to an investor online Q&A platform affiliated with the Shenzhen Stock Exchange, said the quota will be sufficient to ensure normal production at its Kamativi Mine, according to Reuters.

On April 14, Interesting Engineering reported:

‘World-first’ off-grid project combining solar, wind and 5 GWh of battery storage. This is the world’s first off grid facility being built to supply energy to mining operations. Fortescue, an Australia-based metals mining major, is working to eliminate fossil fuel usage by shifting to its off-grid renewable energy hub as early as next year. The facility being built at Fortescue’s Pilbara ore site...

On April 15, Interesting Engineering reported:

Chinese firm aims to launch world’s first mass-producible 500 Wh/kg solid-state battery. The tech reaches energy densities of 260–500 Wh/kg for extended range. Greater Bay Technology, the high-tech incubator backed by China-based automotive giant GAC Group, has reportedly rolled out its first A-sample cells of a proprietary all-solid-state battery.

On April 20, Bloomberg reported:

Cheap batteries are taking over the world’s power grids. Falling costs, rising electricity demand and the Iran War are nurturing a boom in energy storage.

Batteries for stationary energy storage are getting cheaper (source)

On April 20, Seeking Alpha reported:

China's clean energy exports surge in wake of Iran war - Ember. China’s (MCHI) (FXI) exports of solar products, lithium-ion batteries and electric vehicles surged to a record $21.9B in March amid the Iran war, signaling a sharp acceleration in shipments of the country’s key clean energy technologies. The combined total was up 70% from March 2025, according to Nicolas Fulghum of energy-focused think tank Ember.

On April 21, Bloomberg reported:

China’s CATL debuts EV battery with speedy six-minute recharge...The Ningde-based company, better known as CATL, showcased its latest “Shenxing” lithium-iron-phosphate battery that recharges from 10% power to 98% in just six minutes — a development that brings EV charging closer to the speed of filling up the tank of a gasoline vehicle.

On April 22, Miningdotcom reported:

Lithium market to enter deficit until 2035, says Canaccord. The global lithium market is set to enter a near-decade-long deficit as a lack of mine investment weighs on supply of the EV battery metal, according to Canaccord Genuity...Canaccord analysts said they expect to see a “material market deficit” starting in 2026, given that tightening supply has more than offset the weakness in near-term demand. This deficit, they added, could last until 2035. Even if rising lithium prices through 2027-28 could ignite a supply response, that would still fall short of their demand growth forecasts, the analysts said...Canaccord’s outlook assumes no further disruptions in China and elsewhere, which could well extend the deficit beyond the projected period.

Lithium miner news

Albemarle (ALB)

No significant news for the month, but announced Q1, 2026 earnings will be released on May 6, 2026.

Sociedad Quimica y Minera S.A. (SQM), Covalent Lithium (SQM/WES JV) - Wesfarmers [ASX:WES] (OTCPK:WFAFY)

The Mt Holland Lithium Mine and Kwinana Refinery are a 50/50 JV ("Covalent Lithium") between Wesfarmers and SQM, located in Western Australia.

On April 7, Market Screener reported:

Quarterly dividend of USD 0.6692 per share payable on May 26, 2026, ex-date on May 08, 2026 and record date on May 08, 2026.

On April 21, Argus Media reported:

Western Australia extends support for lithium producers...The A$30mn ($21.5mn) pledge to extend fee relief first introduced in 2024 will benefit major Chinese lithium producer Tianqi and Australia's Covalent Lithium until 31 December 2027.

Rio Tinto [ASX:RIO] [LN:RIO] (RIO) - Includes a 53.9% stake in Nemaska Lithium in a JV with the Quebec government

On April 21, Rio Tinto announced:

First quarter 2026 production results. 9% YoY CuEq1 growth from our three value-generating, world-leading businesses...Lithium: Fenix 1B and Sal de Vida achieved mechanical completion as planned, with first production on track for H2 2026.

Rio Tinto Q1, 2026 production results and 2026 guidance (source)

Upcoming catalysts include:

  • 2026 - Plan to expand and ramp up Fenix Mine by 10 ktpa and start and ramp up the Sal de Vida Project to 15 ktpa LCE. The total lithium 2026 production target is 61-64 kt LCE.
  • 2028 - Plans to ramp Rincon up to 25 ktpa, then later build Rincon up to 53 ktpa LCE; and start the Nemaska Lithium Project, then ramp up to 28ktpa LCE.

Note: You can view a nice summary of all Rio's lithium projects and timelines here. Their end 2028 target is 225,000 tpa LCE.

Ganfeng Lithium Group [SHE:002460] [HK:1772] (GNENY)

On April 21, Gasgoo reported:

Benefiting from rising lithium prices, Ganfeng Lithium expects Q1 return to profitability. Ganfeng Lithium released its first-quarter 2026 earnings forecast late on April 16, projecting a return to profitability. The company estimates net profit for the quarter will land between 1.6 billion yuan and 2.1 billion yuan — a surge of 549.65% to 690.17% from a year earlier. Excluding non-recurring items, net profit is expected to reach 1.25 billion yuan to 1.75 billion yuan, climbing 616.12% to 822.56% year-over-year.

(Chengdu) Tianqi Lithium Industries Inc. [SHE:002466], Tianqi Lithium Energy Australia (TLEA) is a JV with Tianqi Lithium (51%) and IGO Limited (49%). TLEA owns the Kwinana lithium hydroxide facility in WA

On the evening of April 20, Tianqi Lithium announced that it expects the net profit attributable to shareholders of the listed company for the first quarter of 2026 to be between Yuan 1.7 billion and 2.0 billion, compared to Yuan 104 million in the same period last year, representing a year-on-year increase of 1530.31% to 1818.01%.

Chengxin Lithium Group Co., Ltd. [SHE:002240] - Also owns Sichuan Zhiyuan Lithium Industry Co., Ltd and is a supplier to BYD Co.

On March 27, Market Screener reported: "Chengxin Lithium says 2025 net loss at 888.1 million yuan."

data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: Strong Pipeline, Weak Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
About a year ago, I published my last article about the pharmaceutical company Bristol Myers Squibb Company (BMY), and I rated the stock as a “Buy” and also wrote that it is one of the better picks in the current market. I also wrote in my conclusion:

We certainly should keep the disappointing study results in mind, but Bristol Myers Squibb raising guidance despite the challenging macroeconomic environment is a good sign in my opinion.

Of course, the risk always remains that the stock will drop even lower – and as I am writing this, investors seem not completely happy with the results and/or the guidance as the stock declined about 2%. But considering the limited risk for pharmaceutical companies in case of a recession and in light of tariffs as well as the low valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for, this is a risk we can take in my opinion. And even if we have to wait before the stock price moves higher, we can at least collect dividends in the meantime and a dividend yield above 5% is certainly tempting.

Since my last article was published, the stock increased slightly above 21% (and when including dividends, investors gained even 27.5% in value). This is without much doubt a very good performance over one year, but we also must point out that Bristol Myers Squibb was probably not one of the better picks (as I wrote in my last article). The S&P 500 actually increased 29.6% in the same timeframe and therefore outperformed Bristol Myers Squibb (although the S&P 500 performance was mostly driven by only a few companies).

In the following article, we will analyze that Bristol Myers Squibb has the potential to grow its revenue in the years to come and is trading for a very cheap valuation multiple. But we also must take into account that analysts are very pessimistic about the business and assume declining revenue for several years in a row. In the end, I will argue that Bristol Myers Squibb is still a solid investment, but not the compelling “Buy” it was a year ago.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the last results for fiscal 2025, which the company did report in early February 2026. For fiscal 2025, Bristol Myers Squibb reported a total revenue of $48,194, resulting in a decline of 0.2% year-over-year compared to $48,300 million in the previous year. While revenue was declining a bit, the company could now report $9,328 million in EBIT again—following $8,379 million in losses before income taxes in fiscal 2024. And instead of a loss per diluted share of $4.41 in fiscal 2024, the company reported earnings per share of $3.46.

Full-year results are certainly not so great, but fourth-quarter results look a bit brighter. Revenue increased from $12,342 million in Q4/24 to $12,502 million in Q4/25, resulting in 1.3% year-over-year growth. EBIT jumped from only $175 million in the same quarter last year to $1,470 million in this quarter, and diluted earnings per share also increased from $0.04 in Q4/24 to $0.53 in Q4/25. In both cases, we must state that Q4/24 was probably an outlier.

Can BMY Still Grow?

In the last few years, management always acknowledged that Bristol Myers Squibb is facing a patent cliff and that several pharmaceuticals will report declining revenue in the next few years. The company labeled these products its legacy portfolio and always made clear that revenue for this portfolio will decline. On the other hand, the company had its growth portfolio consisting of newer products that will drive growth and at least offset declines from the legacy portfolio.

In 2025 we clearly saw the shift from legacy portfolio to growth portfolio continuing. In fiscal 2024, the biggest part of revenue still stemmed from the legacy portfolio, and in fiscal 2025 the biggest part of revenue now stems from the growth portfolio. Nevertheless, the legacy portfolio is still generating 45% of total revenue, and this still represents a significant risk for declining revenues.

And what seems even more troubling is the fact that Bristol Myers Squibb seems to be able to offset declining revenue from its legacy portfolio, but the growth portfolio can’t drive overall revenue higher. For fiscal 2026, total revenue is expected to be between $46.0 billion and $47.5 billion, which would result in low-single-digit declines once again. Diluted earnings per share are also expected to be in a range of $6.05 to $6.35, which would result either in a low single digit decline or low-single-digit growth year-over-year.

Management is only offering guidance for fiscal 2026, but analysts are presenting earnings and revenue expectations for several years to come. Bristol Myers Squibb is usually trying to be optimistic that new products will set off declines, and the business will also be able to grow, but analysts are obviously not buying it.

Revenue outlook for the next few years seems rather grim. It is not surprising that analysts are often cautious about pharmaceutical companies and do not necessarily assume high growth rates in 5 or 10 years from now. Growth is heavily dependent on the ability of these companies to introduce new products to the market and not every new pharmaceutical turns out to be a blockbuster.

However, in case of Bristol Myers Squibb, the analysts are expecting revenue to constantly decline in the next few years. Compared to a revenue of slightly above $48 billion in fiscal 2025, analysts are only expecting $36 billion in revenue in fiscal 2030 – resulting in an annual decline of 5.6%. These predictions are also not based on just one or two analysts, but many different people being quite pessimistic about Bristol Myers Squibb. This is implying that analysts are not really optimistic about the company’s current growth portfolio.

It is also implying that analysts are not really believing in the pipeline and the company’s ability to introduce new additional blockbusters to the market in the next few years. Management is seeing this different and pointing towards its pipeline and is talking about a wave of at least 10 new product launch opportunities until 2030 and is presenting a pipeline with almost 70 candidates.

Among these pipeline candidates, management is pointing towards five more promising candidates – including Cobenfy, Milvexian, Admilparant, Pumitamig and Iberdomide as well as Mezigdomide.

Cobenfy already generated $155 million in fiscal 2025 and $50 million in Q4/25. And with more data being expected in 2027 and 2028 it might be used for many different indications leading to peak sales of $4 billion to $5 billion and about 10 million potential patients. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Cobenfy revenue in the fourth quarter was $51 million, with continued steady uptake among prescribers and patients. Cobenfy's uptake has surpassed all schizophrenia comparators and relevant analogs in the first year of launch, and we continue to expect steady growth throughout the year.

A second promising drug is Milvexian, for which management is expecting about 15 million potential patients. The company is expecting more data in 2026 and analysts are still very optimistic and expect peak sales to be at least $1.5 billion (in the past, analysts have been more optimistic and expected sales to be somewhere between $3 billion and $5 billion).

Finally, we can mention Pumitamig, also known as BNT327, which is developed under a strategic partnership with the German company BioNTech (BNTX). Not only did both companies report promising phase II results, but analysts are also expecting peak sales of $4.5 billion.

All in all, Bristol Myers Squib has the theoretical potential to grow revenue in the years to come and certainly set off the declining sales from its legacy portfolio. But we never know whether these products actually make it to market and what actual sales will be in the years to come.

Dividend

Finally, Bristol Myers Squibb increased its dividend once again – for the 17th consecutive time. And although the dividend was increased only by one cent, the company is now paying $0.63 per share per quarter, which is resulting in an annual dividend of $2.52 and a dividend yield of almost 4.3% making the stock certainly interesting for dividend investors (although the dividend yield has been much higher).

The dividend can also be seen as well covered at this point. When comparing the dividend to the GAAP earnings per share of $3.46, we get a payout ratio of 73%, which is rather high but still acceptable. However, when comparing the dividend to the expected non-GAAP earnings per share of $6.20 for fiscal 2026 (mid-point of guidance), we get a payout ratio of 41%. Finally, we can also compare the amount paid in dividends in fiscal 2025 (about $5.05 billion) to the generated free cash flow ($12.85 billion). This is resulting in a payout ratio of 39% and those metrics are certainly acceptable.

In the years 2019 till 2023, the company also spent high amounts on share buybacks (as high as $8 billion in 2022) and reduced the number of diluted shares outstanding. However, in the last few quarters, the company stopped share repurchase programs.

Balance Sheet

Finally, when considering that analysts are rather pessimistic, we should also look at the company’s balance sheet again. In my last two articles, I did not really take a closer look at the balance sheet, but I think it is important and we should look at the numbers once again.

Let’s start with the good news. The company reduced its total debt from $49,650 million one year earlier to $45,111 million on December 31, 2025. This results in a decrease of 9.1% year-over-year. And the good news is that this was not done by using cash on the balance sheet but by generated free cash flow (which is a good sign).

On December 31, 2025, the company had $2,261 million in short-term debt as well as $42,850 million in long-term debt. When comparing the total debt to the shareholder’s equity of $18,473 million, we get a debt-equity ratio of 2.44, which is rather high. When comparing the total debt to free cash flow of the last four quarters ($12.85 billion) it would take about 3.5 times the free cash flow to repay the debt outstanding. Additionally, we should not ignore $10,673 million in cash and cash equivalents on the balance sheet, that could be used to repay outstanding debt.

If Bristol Myers Squibb is able to grow again, the debt levels are not a problem. However, if analysts are right and the company will rather report declining revenue, these debt levels could become a problem. The business also has $21,754 million in goodwill on its balance sheet – and this amount is exceeding the stockholder’s equity of $18,473 million.

We must not really be worried about the company, its balance sheet and the ability to repay its outstanding debt. But we should keep a close eye on the balance sheet. A combination of declining free cash flow, the debt levels and the high amount of goodwill, could become a problem in the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We can start by looking at the simple valuation multiples, Bristol Myers Squibb was trading for in the last few years. And I am looking only at the last five years and I excluded the previous years as there are some spikes for both valuation multiples that would disturb the picture.

At the time of writing, Bristol Myers Squibb is trading for 17.1 times earnings per share and for 9.4 times free cash flow. And especially the P/FCF ratio is indicating that Bristol Myers Squibb might be undervalued at this point. And although the stock was trading for even lower valuation multiples (only about 5.7 times free cash flow in late 2025), a single valuation multiple still seems to make this a very good investment.

When using a discount cash flow calculation, we come to a similar conclusion. As always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve at least) and with 2,041 million in diluted shares outstanding. As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $12.85 billion. Free cash flow margin is 26.7% right now, which seems to be rather high and clearly above the long-term average of 18.5%. However, in the last ten years, the free cash flow margin was 25.0% on average and in the last five years it was 26.7% and therefore we can argue that the current free cash flow might be reasonable.

Now, let’s assume that Bristol Myers Squibb won’t be able to grow its free cash flow again and assume 0% growth from now till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $62.96 for Bristol Myers Squibb and the stock would be almost fairly valued at this point.

Additionally, we can also make two different calculations – one that is more optimistic, one that is more pessimistic. In a first scenario, we can assume that Bristol Myers Squibb will be able to grow at least in the low single digits in line with GDP growth. When assuming 3% growth annually (and all other assumptions being similar) we get an intrinsic value of $89.94 for Bristol Myers Squibb. This would make the stock still a bargain at this point.

However, we can also calculate by using analysts’ assumptions. As mentioned above, they are expecting revenue to decline about 5% annually for the next few years and assume the business will stabilize in 2030 again. This leads to an intrinsic value of $53.65, and the stock would be overvalued at this point.

Upcoming Quarter

On April 30, 2026, Bristol Myers Squibb will report first quarter results for fiscal 2026, and analysts are expecting revenue to be $10.93 billion, which would result in about a 2.5% year-over-year decline again. Earnings per share (on a GAAP basis) are expected to be $1.15, and compared to the same quarter last year, this would also result in a 5% year-over-year decline.

Analysts are obviously expecting revenue and earnings per share to remain under pressure, and therefore we should pay close attention to the question of whether the growth portfolio is able to outpace the losses from the legacy portfolio. Especially announcements about the pipeline are important and whether Bristol Myers Squibb will be able to introduce additional blockbusters to the market. Right now, the company has several pipeline candidates, but it also needs to convince analysts and investors that these pharmaceuticals can contribute to a growing revenue.

We should also pay attention to the company’s debt levels and whether the business is able to reduce debt levels as well as goodwill.

Conclusion

Determining whether Bristol Myers Squibb is a good investment or not is rather difficult at this point. On the one hand, we have a company with a growth portfolio and a promising pipeline that might not only be able to offset declines from pharmaceuticals losing patent protection but actually grow revenue in the years to come. On the other hand, we have analysts being rather pessimistic and expecting revenue to decline in the mid-single digits in the years to come.

I personally still see Bristol Myers Squibb as a bit undervalued, but it is not a clear “Buy” anymore. The combination of a rather high dividend yield and the very low valuation multiples the stock is trading for still makes it a solid investment, but now there might be better investments in the market.
